column2: Your Name and Company: (What is your name, and what’s your company or organization’s name?)
column3: Contact Details: (Email address and phone number where we can reach you.)
column4: Current Website (if any): (Do you have an existing website? If yes, please provide the URL and briefly note what you like or dislike about it.)
column5: Type of Website/App Needed: (In a few words, describe what you need. For example: “An e-commerce website to sell handmade soaps,” or “A portfolio site to showcase my photography,” or “A mobile-friendly web app for online yoga classes.”)
column6: Primary Goal: What do you hope this website/app will do for your business in the short term and long term? (e.g. “In the short term, attract local customers to book appointments; long term, build an online store for products.”) Please mention any specific objectives or KPIs you have in mind, such as increasing sales, getting more sign-ups, improving brand visibility, etc.​
column7: New or Redesign: Is this a brand new website/app, or a redesign of an existing one? (If it’s a redesign, what are the main reasons you want to redesign? Any current pain points or things you definitely want to change?)
column8: Target Audience: Who are your ideal users or customers? (Describe your target audience in a few lines – for example, “Stay-at-home parents in their 30s looking for fitness routines,” or “Small business owners needing accounting software.” If you have key demographics or customer profiles, share those. Knowing your audience helps tailor the design and features to them.)
column9: Key Actions for Users: What are the top 2-3 things you want visitors to do on your site? (Examples: “Purchase a product,” “Sign up for a newsletter,” “Book a consultation call,” “Browse our portfolio,” “Donate to our cause.” Prioritizing these helps focus the site’s design on driving these actions.)
column10: Branding and Style Guidelines: (Do you have existing brand guidelines or materials? This could be a logo, color scheme, fonts, or a general style guide. If yes, please share them or describe your brand’s look and feel – e.g., “Our brand uses a navy blue and gold palette and a modern, minimalist style.” If no, that’s okay – we will design a style that suits your business. You can also mention any brand personality adjectives, like “professional and trustworthy” or “fun and edgy.”)​
column11: Content Availability: (Do you already have content ready for the site, such as text, images, videos? For example, “We have written content for all pages and have product photos,” or “We need help creating content and sourcing images.” Also mention if you’ll need features like a blog where you’ll regularly add content.)
column12: Inspiration – Likes/Dislikes: (List 2-3 websites or apps that you like the look or functionality of. They can be competitors or from any industry. What do you like about them? e.g., layout, colors, navigation, imagery. Also, if there are any design styles you dislike, feel free to mention those too. This helps ensure we align with your taste.)
column13: Visual Style Preferences: (How would you describe the ideal look and feel of your site? You can choose multiple or add your own: Clean and minimalist, Colorful and vibrant, Corporate and professional, Friendly and casual, Luxury/premium, Modern and trendy, Classic and traditional, etc. Additionally, are there specific colors you want to use or avoid? Any particular imagery or iconography that comes to mind – for instance, “I love nature imagery” or “No stock photos of people, please.”)
column14: Tone of Voice: (If applicable, describe the tone the website’s text should convey: e.g., formal vs. informal, approachable, humorous, authoritative, compassionate, etc. This will guide content if we’re helping create it, or ensure our design matches the tone of your existing content.)
column15: Please check all that apply and provide details where relevant.
column16: Integrations: Do you need the site to connect with any specific tools or services? (Examples: Email newsletter service like Mailchimp, CRM like Salesforce, social media feeds, Google Maps, payment gateways like Stripe or PayPal, video platforms like YouTube/Vimeo, etc. List any that come to mind.)
column17: E-commerce/Transaction Details: (If your site will involve transactions – product sales, bookings, donations – are there any legal/security or other considerations? For instance, “We will need to charge tax for certain states,” or “Our booking requires users to sign a waiver,” or “We want to offer subscription payments.” This helps ensure we plan for the right e-commerce setup.)
column18: Domain Name: (Do you have a domain name registered for this site? If yes, what is it and who is the domain registrar (if known)? If no, do you need help choosing or registering a domain?)
column19: Hosting: (Do you already have a hosting provider/server where the site will live? If yes, who is the host? If no or if you’re not sure, we can recommend hosting options and manage the setup for you.)
column20: Timeline: Do you have a target launch date or timeframe in mind? (Is there an event or deadline driving this project, such as “We need the site live before Black Friday,” or “No hard deadline, but within 3 months would be nice.” Also, if you have any timing constraints on your end, like vacation periods or product launch schedules, please let us know. This helps set a realistic project schedule.)
column21: Budget: Do you have a budget range allocated for this project? (Providing a ballpark figure or range helps us propose solutions that fit your budget. We understand if you prefer not to state a number first, but even an approximate range – e.g., “$5k-$8k” or “under $15k” – is helpful. It ensures we focus on features that are feasible within your budget and suggest alternatives if needed​.)
column22: Maintenance & Updates After Launch: (We offer ongoing maintenance and support plans. Would you be interested in support after the site goes live, such as regular updates, backups, or content changes? For example, “Yes, I’d like monthly maintenance” or “No, I plan to maintain it myself” or “Not sure yet.” This isn’t a final commitment, just to gauge your needs.)
column23: Future Enhancements: (Do you anticipate any features that you don’t need at launch but might want to add later? For instance, “We might add online courses in the future,” or “If this goes well, phase 2 might be a mobile app.” This helps in building a foundation that can expand down the road.)
column24: Biggest Concern or Priority: (What is the most important aspect of this project to you? And is there anything that concerns you? For example, “Making sure the site is very easy to use for non-tech-savvy customers,” or “Staying within budget,” or “Achieving a wow-factor design,” etc. Knowing this helps us focus on what matters most to you.)​
column25: Any Other Notes or Questions: (Feel free to share anything else you think is relevant that we haven’t covered above. Or any questions you have for us about the process or what’s possible. This could be about content, design, technical details, or anything at all.)

column6:
In the short term, I want the app to attract users who are new to budgeting, especially young adults or people trying to get control of their finances. The goal is to help them track spending, set goals, and build better money habits. I’ll measure success through metrics like user sign-ups, app engagement (daily/weekly usage), and feedback/reviews.

Long term, I want the app to become a go-to personal finance tool, expanding into features like debt payoff tracking, investment planning, and financial education. Eventually, I’d like to monetize through premium features or partnerships with financial services.
column8:
This is a brand new app. I’m building it from the ground up to solve a common problem: making budgeting simple, visual, and non-intimidating for everyday users.

The main priority is to create something minimal yet powerful, where users can track income, categorize expenses, and set financial goals without feeling overwhelmed.
column9:
The top 2–3 things I want visitors to do on the site are:

Download the mobile app – The main goal is to drive users to install the budgeting app from the App Store or Google Play.

Understand the app’s value quickly – I want the site to clearly explain how the app helps users take control of their money, ideally through a short demo, visuals, or feature breakdown.

Join the email list for updates – For users not ready to download yet, I want them to sign up for tips, financial content, or early access to new features—helping build trust and grow a community over time.
column10:
I don’t have official brand guidelines yet, but I have a vision for the look and feel. I want the brand to feel clean, modern, and approachable—something that makes budgeting feel less intimidating and more empowering. I’m leaning toward a soft blue and white color palette, with simple, rounded fonts that feel friendly and easy to read.

The overall vibe should be minimalist but helpful, with a tone that’s clear, encouraging, and down-to-earth—like a financially-savvy friend guiding you, not a cold institution.

If it helps, brand personality words would be: relatable, trustworthy, encouraging, and simple
column11:
Right now, I don’t have all the content ready. I’ll need help creating some of the written copy for the site—especially for explaining the app’s features and benefits in a simple, clear way. I’ll also need help sourcing images or graphics that match the brand vibe (clean, modern, and approachable).

Eventually, I’d like to have a blog or resource section where I can share budgeting tips, financial advice, and app updates. But for now, the focus is just on launching a solid landing page that drives downloads and builds interest.

column13:
I want the site to feel clean, modern, and friendly. The ideal look is minimalist and approachable, something that feels trustworthy without being too formal or corporate. I’m aiming for a design that’s simple to navigate and easy on the eyes.

For colors, I prefer soft blues and whites with maybe an accent color like mint or light teal. I want to avoid anything too dark or overly saturated. In terms of imagery, I like the idea of using simple illustrations or icons to represent features rather than stock photos of people.

The goal is to make users feel calm, in control, and motivated to take charge of their financ
column14:
The tone should be informal, clear, and approachable. I want the text to feel like a real person talking, not like a financial institution. It should be encouraging and down-to-earth, with a focus on making users feel confident about managing their money—even if they’re just getting started.

Think: helpful, supportive, and friendly, but still professional enough to be taken seriously. No overly technical language or jargon. Just simple, relatable guidance that makes budgeting feel doable.
column15: User Registration / Login;Client Dashboard / Account Area;Blog / News / Articles;Contact Form;Newsletter Signup / Email Capture;Push Notifications;Admin Panel or Content Management System (CMS);SEO Optimization Tools;Analytics & Tracking Setup (e.g., Google Analytics);Custom Forms / Surveys / Questionnaires;Secure Payment Integration (Stripe, PayPal, etc.);Simple budget calculator, Future intergration with bank API, Habit tracker
column16:
Yes, there are a few tools I’d like the site to connect with:

Email marketing: I’m considering using Mailchimp or ConvertKit to manage newsletters and updates.

Analytics: I’ll need Google Analytics to track user behavior and conversions.

Payment gateway: I’d like the option to integrate Stripe or PayPal in the future for premium features or digital product sales.

Push notifications: For the mobile app, I’ll eventually want to use Firebase Cloud Messaging or a similar tool.

Bank integration (future): I’m exploring tools like Plaid for automatic expense tracking, but that will be in a later phase.
column17:
While the core app will be free to start, I do plan to introduce premium features or digital product sales in the future. These may include things like downloadable budget templates, advanced tools, or financial planning resources.

I’ll need support for secure one-time payments and eventually subscription billing (monthly or yearly). I’d like to use Stripe or PayPal for processing, with the ability to manage user subscriptions within the app or site.

At this stage, there’s no need for charging tax or legal waivers, but that may change depending on how products or services evolve. Security and compliance (like SSL, secure checkout, and user data protection) will be important from day one.
column18: Yes, I currently have a domain name registered, but it doesn’t fit the direction of this budgeting app. I’m open to choosing a new domain that better matches the brand and purpose of the app. I’ll likely need help coming up with a name that’s simple, memorable, and available. I’m not attached to any registrar yet, so I’m flexible on where it's registered.
column19: I don’t have a hosting provider yet and I’m open to recommendations. I’d prefer something that’s reliable, secure, and beginner-friendly, with good support and room to grow as the app scales. If possible, I’d also like help managing the setup so everything runs smoothly from the start.
column20: There’s no hard deadline, but I’d like to have a working version of the site and app ready within the next 4 to 6 months. That would give me time to test things, gather early feedback, and start building a user base. I don’t have any major scheduling conflicts coming up, so I’m available to move forward consistently during that timeframe.
column21:
I don’t have a firm number locked in yet, but I’d like to keep the initial build as cost-effective as possible, especially since this is an early-stage project. I’m currently thinking in the range of $3,000 to $7,000 for an MVP (minimum viable product) that includes the website and basic app functionality.

If costs go beyond that, I’m open to phasing things out—starting small and scaling up over time as the app gains traction. I’m also open to hearing suggestions that can stretch the budget further through no-code tools, open-source platforms, or simplified design.
column22: Yes, I’d like monthly maintenance and support after the site goes live. I don’t plan to handle updates, backups, or technical changes myself, so I’m looking for a reliable plan that covers those tasks on an ongoing basis. It’s important to me that the site stays secure, up-to-date, and running smoothly without me needing to manage it directly.
column23:
Yes, there are several features I’d like to add in future phases, even if they’re not part of the initial launch:

Bank account integration (using something like Plaid) so users can track spending automatically

Subscription billing for premium features or digital resources

A full web dashboard for users who prefer managing finances on a desktop

Gamified progress tracking, like streaks or milestones for saving and budgeting

Push notifications to remind users of goals or spending limits

Possibly even financial education content like videos, blog posts, or interactive challenges

I want to start with a strong foundation that can scale over time, so I’d like the app and site to be built in a way that makes it easier to add these features later.
column24:
The most important aspect of this project is making sure the app is simple and easy to use—especially for people who aren’t great with tech or budgeting. I want users to open the app and instantly feel like they can manage their money without feeling overwhelmed.

My biggest concern is staying within budget while still building something that looks polished and functions well. I also want to make sure the design feels clean, modern, and trustworthy, so people feel confident using it with their financial information.
